text.title: Usability Testing — Task Design and Severity
text.summary: Rules for writing valid test tasks and applying the severity rating scale to findings.

section.title: Task Writing Rules

rule.statement: Use scenario framing before the task instruction: give the user a realistic motivation before stating what to accomplish.
rule.rationale: Decontextualized tasks ("find the settings page") feel artificial. Scenario framing activates realistic decision-making.

rule.statement: Never include UI element names, menu labels, or step sequences in a task.
rule.rationale: Including solution steps measures comprehension of instructions, not usability of the interface.

section.p: Task template: Scenario: [context]. Task: [what to accomplish]. Success criteria: [how to measure].

rule.statement: Use moderated sessions for complex, exploratory research where follow-up probing is needed; use unmoderated for validation at scale.
rule.rationale: Moderated sessions surface the why behind behavior; unmoderated provides statistical breadth. Choosing the wrong format wastes budget.

list.item: Moderated: facilitator present, can probe, fewer sessions, higher cost, deeper insight.
Unmoderated: remote automated, pre-set questions only, scales to many sessions, lower cost, broader patterns.

antipattern.description: Helping a user who is stuck by pointing at the correct area or saying "try the menu".
antipattern.reason: Any help makes the finding invalid. Record the failure; do not rescue it.

antipattern.description: Recruiting participants who do not match the target user profile.
antipattern.reason: Results reflect the recruited population, not the actual user base. Wrong participants produce misleading findings.

antipattern.description: Collecting only qualitative observations without any quantitative metrics.
antipattern.reason: Purely subjective findings are difficult to prioritize and impossible to track over time. Always measure task success rate and time on task.
